sentences.text: Yes, that's an exciting part of the story, thank you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I learned many years later how this very confusing last few days came about.
Count Folke Bernadotte, B-E-R-N-A-D-O-T-T-E, he-- he was the head of the Swedish Red Cross and a member of the Swedish royal family.
He wrote the book It may not be in the libraries anymore, but possibly.

He went to plead with Himmler during the last terrible days of the war, in Germany, for the release of the Scandinavian prisoners out of Ravensbruck concentration camp, of which there were a few hundred.
Danish, Norwegian prisoners particularly, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm not sure about Finnish, but could be.
In any event, Himmler wouldn't hear of it and told him, let them all die.
And-- but Himmler remained in-- I'm sorry, but Bernadotte remained in Berlin and about a week later, Himmler could really see which way the war was going, and he decided to save himself.
So he sent an emissary to get Bernadotte and he met-- the-- the two met in the wee hours of the morning in some dilapidated cafe.
Very interestingly, he describes how haggard and exhausted and dirty Himmler looked.
Himmler wanted Bernadotte to be the go-between on a peace between England and Germany.
But Bernadotte turned him down.
He said, no, this will have to be an overall peace treaty, not a piecemeal.
And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Bernadotte took this opportunity to tell Himmler that, do yourself some good, Himmler, because when the Allies come, they're going to string you up.
Release those prisoners.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whereupon Himmler said, take them all out of Ravensbruck, not only the Scandinavian prisoners that he went for.
And so it happened that Bernadotte didn't even ask his government for permission to bring us all in.

He instead telephoned, or by some means got in touch with them quickly, and he asked them to send into northern Germany, to Ravensbruck concentration camp, vehicles that could carry the maximum amount of people-- ambulances, trucks, vans, buses, and so on-- and have them all painted white with red crosses on them, so that the Allies will not bomb them.
And so it happens-- happened that this rescue mission was called the White Fleet Rescue Mission.

They pulled into Ravensbruck concentration camp and they filled up-- them up with prisoners, and-- and he described that some of them were so sick they couldn't-- had to be carried onto ambulances-- ambulances and these vehicles-- and they were dying right there.
And they were dying while-- while they were in the vehicles [CRYING] and I just remember these very, very real scenes.
And while they were free already, they were dying, never had a chance to live their lives fully.

And they-- and I thought that we were then taken to the harbor, but a few years ago, I learned that-- through research that, in fact, we were dropped on the Danish side, and these white-- the white fleet returned to carry more-- more of these inmates, still.

The white fleet rescue mission caught up with our train and-- and went from camp to camp nearby until it was full, and we were attached to the rescue mission, and we were liberated and taken to-- to Denmark.
I was then carried on a stretcher onto-- out of this vehicle, and I told them I can walk, but they said to lie down.
And I was very good at taking orders still in those days.
And then we were taken to a ferry boat, and we crossed the body of water separating Denmark from Sweden called the Oresund.
And we crossed the Oresund to Malmo, Sweden, where they fumigated us with DDT, and we showered.
We received new clothes and we started a new life there.
And since we numbered into the thousands, they separate-- separated us into the various communities.
They-- the distributed us into the various communities of the area, and I was sent to Landskrona, which is a charming little town in southern Sweden.
From there you could actually see across the Oresund over to Copenhagen-- [SWEDISH], as we call it in Swedish.
And there we-- there in Landskrona we were-- they let the schools out early to make room for all of us.
They placed mattresses on the floors of the classes and we-- there were nurses and doctors.
And we were very-- going through a process of examination to separate the contagious-- those who had contagious diseases from those of us who had other serious ailments.
And I was very happy to learn that I had no contagious disease and that-- but just vitamin deficiency problems and 17-- 17 cavities in my teeth needed immediate care.
That was-- my leg was as a result of vitamin-- this-- this vitamin deficiency problem, which really refused to heal for months and months.
They were-- until my body received enough nutrition, it-- it refused to heal.
It-- it took approximately seven, eight months for them to heal.
I still have-- I call them volcanoes-- on my right leg, these-- these-- these marks and bumps where-- where I-- where these scars were.
Yes.
Arrived-- I arrived in Malmo on May 3, 1945.
Now in Malmo, we were quarantined.
I was-- in different high schools, and I was in the [? orka ?] skola in Landskrona.
There was a 25-feet double fence area, beyond which the population could not-- not go.
And so it seemed that they were very curious about us, and they lined the outer fences to look at us.
They learned about us in the newspapers and the radio, at the time.

And one of these people that came to visit-- later, when I got up, I-- I met some of these people-- and there were actually two families that wanted to adopt me, because once I felt better-- I-- I didn't feel sick, I just felt that I needed to rest and I needed to drink and eat.
In fact, for a whole month, we had nothing but oatmeal.
Oh, I-- it-- I was very, very emaciated.
I used to know it, but I don't remember anymore.
I was very, very thin.

I remember, if I may back-- go back a step to Malmo, we were taken to-- to this beautiful high school with an Olympic-sized swimming pool where we showered, received new clothes, and as I mentioned, we were sprayed with DDT first.
And it-- these showers-- they were multi-headed.
They-- they were large rooms with shower heads, and we were afraid to go in there.
We were afraid because to us that meant maybe gas will come out.
We just had a very hard time to get used to normal situations.
It had to be proven to us.
And somebody yelled, well, how do we know that they're not gas?
So they turned the water and after that, we just went in there.
And-- but there was a full length mirror.
I'll never forget the sight when I saw myself.
That's the first thing I wanted to do.
[CRYING] I-- I just didn't think it was me.
I looked at the mirror, and I thought, this is not [?
Hansee, ?] my nickname.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I-- I just couldn't believe that was me.
I remember I-- I just covered myself, like this, as if I want to hide.
How can I hide?
It seemed as though my eyes were huge and my face was tiny and my hair was just so-- wouldn't grow.
Remember, for 13 months, I-- 13 months before, I was shaven bald, and my hair didn't grow very much, and it was full of white lice eggs.
That was-- for a long time-- even after the DDT and-- and other rinses-- the eggs of the lice kept hanging on.
They were dead, but they just hung onto the hair.
I remember when I-- someone gave-- someone gave me a gift.
It was a little comb.
Well, it was a-- a comb, almost-- a little oblong comb, actually, and it has very fine tooth comb to-- to comb out the-- the dead eggs, which I complained about.
I don't want those dead lice eggs in my hair, and they just wouldn't even go through that.
It took so long-- much, much, much brushing-- before-- before I could get rid of my lice.
It's a wonder that I didn't catch typhus.
There's just thousands of these horrible creatures just sucking at your blood.
It-- how can they reduce people to that level?
You know, and they call us untermenschen, well, they are-- the people who-- who-- who did this to us, they are the untermenschen.
Not the people who had to suffer through it.
How can they treat people like that?
They really reduced us to subhuman level, but we were civilized, fine human beings when we entered the camps.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We-- those of us who survived, I don't think we'll ever get over the scars of the Holocaust.
Never.
I think of it all the time and I thank God that I live in this wonderful country, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the United States, where I can enjoy true freedom.
I think of what happened to me many times.

And I think of all the people in the world who have been-- [CRYING] who have not been so fortunate as I am, to live in freedom, and I hope that someday the world is going to make a strong policy to adopt freedom in their land, because that's the only way, the only way that we can be human beings and we can do the most for our country.
For ourselves and for the country and for the world, just-- through freedom.
But I'd like to go back Sweden-- to Sweden.
I-- I cannot put into words the wonderful people the Swedish people are.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Swedes really were there when we needed them.
They couldn't do enough for us.
They were kind, gentle, generous in every way, in every way.
Although-- although, I went back and did some research several times to Malmo, I wanted to know what the news was during that year-- during the period when I was incarcerated.
And also, what did the people think about us coming to Sweden?
And once we got there, what-- what was it that they thought about us?
And of course, they had concerns.
They were concerned, according to some of these articles, that we will bring in these dreadful diseases for which they have no cure, and suppose it spreads to the population.
How will they deal with it?
These were very real problems and I couldn't blame them.
We were totally strangers and it's true, Sweden was not involved in the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not directly.
The-- the German army had to go through Sweden to go to-- get to Norway, but they had to promise that they let them through, otherwise Sweden had-- would have been taken, as well.
All the time that I was in Sweden.
However, while I was in quarantine, after a few days, I began to go out-- outside to the yard-- there was a big yard-- and I saw lots of bicycles parked there.
And I asked the doctor if I could use a bicycle.
And he said, oh, yes.
All the nurses and the doctors come by bicycle, and I should pick any I want, and I could ride around the school yard.
How wonderful, I said.
I had a bicycle in Czechoslovakia, and-- and I inherited my-- Victor's bicycle.
By the way, I'd like to say something about Victor later, which I-- and about the entire family-- who survived and where they went and how they were rescued, before-- please remind me.
And so there I was, riding around the schoolyard.
I was 15 at this time, and this is how I met the Swedish family who came to visit me regularly after that.
They inquired about me through the doctor, the Swedish doctor, as to what shape am I in?
And the doctor said, she'll be all right, that she-- she just needs to be rehabilitated but that she will be all right.
How did they know about you?
Well, they just saw me on a bicycle riding around the schoolyard.
And this was so wonderful.
I did it for fun because I had to use a bicycle in the summer to go to school, and spring and whenever the weather permitting.
Otherwise, I had to take the train in the winter time.
That was back home, of course.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they started sending me Swedish letters, and I couldn't read them but some of the nurses translated it to German, and so, slowly, I-- I could understand it.
And with the help of the inmates, we answered in German to the Swedish family, and eventually, they asked me if I would like to become a daughter-- a sister to their daughter, Gulan.
That's her name, Gulan.
She was one year younger than I, and it was so nice for me to meet someone close to my age, who had a so-called normal life.
Eventually, when they asked me to become their daughter, I wrote to them that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am in a state of mind still that I believe that perhaps some of my family have survived.
I don't want to be adopted, but I would love to come and live with them.
And once the Swedish Red Cross permitted me to be released, I could go and live with them.
But after quarantine, we-- we went to another-- we were sent to another camp for rehabilitation.
We were continually under doctor-- doctor's care.
I was the youngest member of that transport, by the way, to Sweden.
It was established when I got there.
There were some-- there were just really a couple of other girls who were a few months older, or maybe a year older, than I was.
There was one girl from Czechoslovakia was only about six, seven months older than I was.
But all the others were older.
And later, when I returned to Malmo, I went to visit the cemetery, and there were rows and rows of gravestones.
And I have-- I've-- we filmed these stones-- 60 millimeter of film-- we have them.
And some-- some stones give their first names, others both, and if they had the date of birth, they-- they have it on it, and where they are from.
But they all give limited bits of information about them and there were many more than I-- than I really thought.
Eventually, a monument was put up in-- in the cemetery for the victims of the Holocaust from the European countries that were affected.
It's a beautiful monument, right next to the synagogue-- well, across the street from the synagogue in Malmo, Sweden.
The people who were buried there were those who were rescued by Count Folke Bernadotte, and they just didn't-- didn't make it.
They died on the way or after.
Oh, the Swedish family was just superb.
My Swedish foster father, we-- I called him Farbror Eric.
Farbror means-- 'far' is part of the word, means father, Eric is his first name-- and it designates that he's father's brother.
So I called it-- it's-- it's equivalent to an uncle.
So Farbror Eric.

I couldn't call anybody mother or father because in my-- deep down in my mind, I felt that some of my family must have survived and until-- that-- and that nobody can take that place or their places, and therefore, I-- we can get close another way, and they accepted it.
Ah, Eric Berglund, my foster father and Lilly Berglund, my foster mother-- he died, by the way, a few years ago-- and their daughter is Gulan Berglund who eventually married a Jewish man.

And when I visited her the first time, that was in 1962, she showed me her kosher kitchen, [LAUGHS] and taught me-- well, wanted me to know which is the towel to be used for [HEBREW] and which towel to use for [HEBREW].
And when I lived with her, I went with her to church, and she came with me to the synagogue, and-- and we had a wonderful, wonderful relationship.
And we call each other sisters to date.
I was with them for two years until-- and while I was with them, I found out about my American relatives.
My mother's sister-- two sisters and brother came to the United States before World War I.
I believe I mentioned it earlier, so excuse me.
And they married and had children, so actually, I had a large family in the United States, and this is the family that stayed intact.
My European family was decimated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] While I was with the Swedish family, one night, I dreamt my uncle's address in St.
Louis, Missouri.
I used to know his address, everybody's addresses, because I mailed mother's letters, and I used to write little notes under her letter.
I wanted to be close.
And they sent me beautiful things, which permitted me to dress nicely while I was in Europe-- unusual clothes.
And so-- but I forgot.
After the Holocaust, I was totally blank.
I couldn't remember anything more than their names and the city in which they lived in.
But we didn't have a 911.
We didn't have a 411.
We didn't have the directory and the operator assistance that we have today.
God bless this industrial revolution.
[LAUGHS] And there was no way that I could find out what-- what their addresses were.
Furthermore, I had no idea whether the United States was bombed to pulp, as well.
I had no idea, since I was completely blank.
We were uninformed about the situation.
Of course, we knew that the US won, the-- the Allies won the war, but the details were missing still.
So one night-- Gulan and I shared one room in twin beds and I woke up.
I said, Gulan, Gulan, I just dreamt that my uncle's address was such and such Del Mar, St.
Louis, Missouri.
She says, write it down, because by tomorrow morning, you won't remember it.
And I wondered where she-- how wise she was that she made me do that.
And I just dutifully wrote it down.
She handed me pencil and paper, and the following morning, she commanded me to write a letter.
So I figured, well, I have nothing to lose, maybe-- maybe this is it.
And sure enough, my uncle received my letter, and I missed it by two numbers within the same block.
And of course, he contacted my aunt-- my other aunt was-- died in the meantime, and the whole family throughout the country, and I was the first one to show a sign of life.
They had no idea about my mother, and of course, I didn't, either, and I wrote this to him in the letter.
And this is an uncle who came home with his wife and daughter, who is just 20 days younger than I am.
And when they came home to what was Czechoslovakia at the time, we set up the monument for my grandpa, my uncle's father, in the cemetery, because he died when I was six months old.
So we dedicated the monument in the cemetery, and that picture I found in an American relative's album, which I will submit to you today for-- for this.
I didn't know that the war was finished until I got to Sweden.

When we reached-- I remember that, in Copenhagen, I still saw German soldiers, with guns on their shoulders, walking back and forth at the train station because the war was still on, it was just the tail end.
It was five days before the war ended when I was liberated, you see.
But then, remember, maybe some of the other women knew, but I was in very bad shape and I was probably preoccupied with my well-being or with my problems, rather.
Well, I asked the Swedish family to help me locate my family.
I started writing to the Czech address, the same place we had our house-- we had a seven-room house and a big barn.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes, to the Czech address, Hungarian address, and eventually, I had a letter, months and months after I was liberated, that my mother received six letters from me at the same time, written by me over a period of time.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is the way I learned that they are alive.
